title: Unix Knowledge Test
description: Test your understanding of Unix with 25 questions.

questions.question: On a Unix host, logs live under /var/logs/app/** and rotate hourly into .log and .log.gz. Each line is either 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS LEVEL: text' or JSON with fields ts, service, lvl, msg. Multi-line traces start with TRACE: and indented lines. Write a robust one-liner (no external deps beyond standard UNIX tools) that prints for each service the top 2 most frequent ERROR messages observed in the last 12 hours as '<service>:<message>:<count>'. Distinctness by case-insensitive message; ignore traces; skip unreadable files and archives?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question evaluates practical log-analysis skills, focusing on the ability to handle mixed log formats, compressed files, multi-line traces, and time-window filtering. It emphasizes the creation of efficient streaming pipelines and robust error handling using standard Unix tools.

## Key Concepts
- **Standard Tools**: Proficiency with `find`, `zcat`, `awk`, `sed`, `sort`, and `uniq`.
- **Stream Processing**: Designing pipelines that operate without temporary files.
- **Data Normalization**: parsing timestamps, extracting messages, and deduplicating content across varied formats.
- **Robustness**: Handling unreadable files and archives gracefully.

## Code Example
```bash
find /var/log/app/ -type f \( -name '*.log' -o -name '*.log.gz' \) -print0 | xargs -0 zgrep -h "ERROR" ...
```

questions.question: On a Unix host, backups are stored under /backups/<service>/ with files named backup-YYYYMMDD-HHMMSS(.gz). Some services have many files; write a robust one-liner (no external deps beyond standard UNIX tools) that prints, for every service, the path to its most recently modified backup file that is not older than 7 days. Ignore unreadable files?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests comfort with shell one-liners, per-directory aggregation, and robust error handling in a realistic backup-check task.

## Key Concepts
- Per-directory data aggregation
- find -mtime and -printf usage
- Quoting for spaces and 2>/dev/null for unreadable files

## Code Example
```bash
for d in /backups/*/; do [ -d "$d" ] || continue; f=$(find "$d" -maxdepth 1 -type f -mtime -7 -printf '%T@ %p\n' 2>/dev/null | sort -n | tail -n1 | cut -d' ' -f2-); if [ -n "$f" ]; then echo "$d: $f"; fi; done
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you modify to include file size and human-readable date?
- How would you integrate this into a cron job with logging?

find /tmp /tmp/$USER -type f -mtime -7 -perm /002 -printf '%p\t%M\n' 2>/dev/null

questions.explanation:
This question assesses practical Unix shell skills for auditing world-writable files in /tmp, which is critical for preventing unintended data leakage.

**Key Concepts**

- **Permission matching**: `-perm /002` matches any file with the world-writable bit set
- **Time filtering**: `-mtime -7` limits results to files modified within the last 7 days
- **Safe output**: `-printf` provides controlled formatting; `%p` safely handles spaces in filenames
- **Error suppression**: `2>/dev/null` silently skips unreadable files

**Code Breakdown**

```
find /tmp /tmp/$USER    # Search in both locations
  -type f               # Match regular files only
  -mtime -7             # Modified within the last 7 days
  -perm /002            # Has world-writable bit set
  -printf '%p\t%M\n'    # Output: path + tab + permissions
  2>/dev/null           # Suppress permission errors
```

## Why This Is Asked

Tests practical UNIX troubleshooting: /proc inspection, FD counting, and portable shell logic.

## Key Concepts

- /proc/[pid]/fd enumerates open FDs; 2>/dev/null handles perms
- /proc/[pid]/cmdline uses NUL separators; convert to spaces

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you adapt for containers/namespaces?
- How would you optimize for systems with thousands of PIDs?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to handle mixed log formats, compression, time-window filtering, and robust file handling in production-like Unix environments.

## Key Concepts
- Recursive file discovery and safe error handling
- Decompressing gzip on-the-fly with zcat
- Parsing mixed line formats (plain text vs JSON)
- Time window calculations using epoch timestamps
- Aggregation by IP with awk and final sorting

## Code Example
```bash
find /var/log/serv-*/access-logs -type f \( -name '*.log' -o -name '*.log.gz' \) -print0 |
  xargs -0 -I{} sh -lc 'case "$1" in *.gz) zcat "$1" ;; *) cat "$1" ;; esac' -- {} |
  awk 'BEGIN{now=systime()}'
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you adapt for a sliding 24h window with overlapping rotations?
- How would you test correctness with synthetic logs and corrupted lines?
- How would you optimize for very large log sets across multiple files and hosts?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests the candidate's ability to combine mixed log formats, handle multi-line event boundaries, and perform time-windowed frequency analysis using only core Unix tools.

## Key Concepts
- Parsing text and JSON log lines
- Decompressing .gz archives on the fly
- Aggregating multi-line blocks as single events
- Time-window filtering (last 48 hours)
- Frequency counting and top-N extraction

## Code Example
```bash
find /var/log/app-collect -type f \( -name '*.log' -o -name '*.log.gz' \) -print0 \
  | xargs -0 -I{} sh -c 'zcat {} 2>/dev/null || cat {}' \
  | awk -vsince="$(date -d '48 hours ago' '+%s')" '
    /^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}/ { ts=$1" "$2; gsub(/[:-]/," ",ts); if((s=mktime(ts))>since) 
      { if($3=="ERROR:") msg=substr($0,index($0,$4)); else if($0~/^  /) next }
    }
    /^"/ { next }
    {"lvl"} && $0~/ERROR/ { getline j; if(j~"msg") msg=j }
    msg { count[msg]++ }
    END { n=asort(count,c); for(i=n; i>n-5 && i>0; i--) print c[i] }
  '
```
This solution handles both text and JSON formats, treats multi-line blocks as single events, filters by timestamp, and outputs the top 5 ERROR messages.

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Assesses practical use of core Unix tools to summarize file metadata across a directory tree, including compressed files, while gracefully handling unreadable files.

## Key Concepts
- find with -mtime and -type f
- -printf date formatting (%TY-%Tm-%Td)
- sort/uniq for per-day counting
- awk for custom formatting
- 2>/dev/null to silence errors

## Code Example
```bash
find /var/log/containers -type f -mtime -5 -printf '%TY-%Tm-%Td\n' 2>/dev/null | sort | uniq -c | awk '{printf "%s: %d\\n", $2, $1}'
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you adjust for BSD/macOS where -printf may be missing? 
- How would you handle spaces or special characters in filenames safely?

  ...

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Detecting FD leaks is a common production pitfall. This tests precise, minimal tooling and how candidates navigate /proc while avoiding noisy artifacts like sockets.

## Key Concepts
- /proc structure and permissions
- Counting FDs per process and filtering streams
- Resolving symlinks with readlink
- Edge cases: unreadable fds, permission errors, non-regular files

## Code Example
```bash
# Minimal approach sketch (not full production-ready)
for pid in /proc/[0-9]*; do
  if [ -r "$pid/fd" ] && [ "$(stat -c %U "$pid")" != "root" ]; then
    count=$(ls -1 "$pid/fd" 2>/dev/null | wc -l)
    targets=()
    for fd in "$pid/fd/")"; do
      t=$(readlink -f "$fd" 2>/dev/null)
      case "$t" in
        socket:*|pipe:*|*anon_inode*) continue ;;
        *) targets+=("$t") ;;
      esac
    done
    printf "%s\t%d\t%s\n" "$(basename "$pid")" "$count" "${targets[*]:0:3}"
  fi
done
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you adapt to high churn, ensuring accurate counts without races?
- How would you incorporate this into a periodic health check without spawning heavy processes?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to combine find, awk, and sort to compute per-extension disk usage with realistic constraints (symlinks, unreadable files, nested dirs, last-24h window).

## Key Concepts
- find -L -mtime -1 -size +0 for time, size, and symlinks
- basename/extension parsing in awk
- streaming aggregation and top-k output

## Code Example

```bash
find /data/uploads -L -type f -mtime -1 -size +0 -printf '%s\t%p\n' 2>/dev/null | \
awk 'BEGIN{FS="\\t"} {split($2,pp,"/"); f=pp[length(pp)]; split(f,extParts,"."); ext=extParts[length(extParts)]; if(ext!="") sum[ext]+=$1} END{for(e in sum) printf("%s\\t%s\\n", e, sum[e])}' | \
sort -k2 -rn | head -3
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you adapt this to output human-readable sizes (KB/MB/GB)?
- How would you modify to cap memory usage for extremely large datasets?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests ability to normalize heterogeneous log formats into a single, streamable standard while handling compression and rotations. It also probes deduplication, timestamp normalization, and ordering under real-world constraints.

## Key Concepts

- Robust format detection (plain vs JSON)
- On-the-fly decompressing of gzip logs
- Timestamp normalization to a common ISO8601
- Time-window filtering (last 60 minutes)
- Deduplication across rotated files

## Code Example

```bash
# Example approach (not executable as-is):
for f in /logs/app-logs/*/log-*.log /logs/app-logs/*/log-*.log.gz; do
  [ -f "$f" ] || continue
  case "$f" in
    *.gz) zcat "$f" ;; *) cat "$f" ;; esac
  fi
done | \
awk 'BEGIN{now=systime(); win=3600}# ...
''
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you scale this across multiple hosts?
- How to integrate with a central sink (e.g., Kafka) while preserving order?

questions.explanation:
## Diagnosis Steps
- Use `top` or `htop` to identify the high CPU process and its PID
- Run `ps aux` to view process details and command line arguments
- Monitor system calls with `strace -p PID` to understand process behavior

## Investigation Tools
- `lsof -p PID` reveals open files and network connections
- `perf top` provides CPU performance profiling and bottleneck identification
- `/proc/PID/status` contains comprehensive memory and CPU statistics

## Resolution
- Send SIGTERM (`kill -15`) for graceful process shutdown
- Use SIGKILL (`kill -9`) only when the process is unresponsive
- Analyze logs in `/var/log/` to identify and address the root cause

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question assesses the ability to craft a robust, dependency-free one-liner that handles mixed input formats, multi-line blocks, and gzip-compressed files while enforcing a precise 3-hour time window and per-host/per-service aggregation.

## Key Concepts
- Stream processing with awk for multi-format inputs
- Time-window filtering without external tools
- Handling gzip-compressed logs in a single pipeline
- Deduplication with composite keys (host, service, message, stack)

## Code Example
```bash
gzip -cd /logs/**/*.log.gz 2>/dev/null | awk '/^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}/{ts=$1" "$2} /^"ts":/{getline ts; gsub(/"/,"",ts)} /"lvl":"ERROR"/{ok=1} ok{print FILENAME,ts,$0} /^[ ]*$/,/^[A-Z]/{if(/^STACK:/||/^[ ]+[^" ]/){stack=stack RS $0}} END{print host,svc,msg,stack}' | sort -u
```
Note: Replace host and svc extraction logic with your log's naming convention.
